coredata.dc:title: Impact of carbon dots on the structural, morphological, magnetic, and electrochemical performance of Zn ferrite for energy storage applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Sulaimi, Sulaiman
Jahan, M. Qamar
Ahmad, Mukhtar
Batool, Zahra
Hamza, Muhammad
Rehman, Atiq ur
Ashraf, Ghulam Abbas
Yang, Jin
Al-Ammar, Essam A.
Wabaidur, Saikh Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Magnetic core-shell Zn ferrite and carbon dots (C-dots) composite have been synthesized in this work using a simple coprecipitation and hydrothermal process, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns demonstrate an inverse spinel structure of Zn ferrites; the lattice parameter has decreased from 8.19 Å to 8.10 Å with C-dots. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images reveal rods-like structures for C-dots, and in the composite case, it is uniformly dispersed using low contrast consecutive C-dot layers to produce a core-shell structure. Zn ferrites are non-uniform in size, whereas C-dots and their composites are in uniform shape according to SEM images. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) study indicates that the substitution has been successful. For Zn ferrites, and composite, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra shows that the absorption band of the tetrahedral site at 990 cm−1, and 870 cm−1, respectively. Different bonds in the infrared spectrum match specific function groups and bonding in various chemicals. The vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) results indicates that the composite displays ferrimagnetic behavior. The specific capacity for composite has been found to be 2351 and 1790 F/g using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements, respectively. Higher electrochemical performance of the prepared composites may be helpful for energy storage systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc ferrites
Carbon dots
Inverse spinel
Ferrimagnetic behavior
Energy storage devices

EDX Spectra for (a) Zinc Ferrites and (b) carbon dots and (c) composite.

M − H loop of ZnF
e 2
O 4
and C-dots/ZnF
e 2
O 4
composite.
(a) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement of ZFO at different values of scan rate (3–40 mV/s) (b) CV measurements taken at various scan rates (3–40 mV/s) for ZFO@C-dots
(A) GCD curves for ZFO at various current densities ranging from 2 to 3.4 A/g having a potential window of 0.6 V (B) Shows GCD curves for ZFO@C-dots at different current densities (2–3.4 A/g).

Vibrational parameters of ZnFe2O4 and C-dots composite.

head.ce:title: Impact of carbon dots on the structural, morphological, magnetic, and electrochemical performance of Zn ferrite for energy storage applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetic core-shell Zn ferrite and carbon dots (C-dots) composite have been synthesized in this work using a simple coprecipitation and hydrothermal process, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns demonstrate an inverse spinel structure of Zn ferrites; the lattice parameter has decreased from 8.19 Å to 8.10 Å with C-dots. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images reveal rods-like structures for C-dots, and in the composite case, it is uniformly dispersed using low contrast consecutive C-dot layers to produce a core-shell structure. Zn ferrites are non-uniform in size, whereas C-dots and their composites are in uniform shape according to SEM images. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) study indicates that the substitution has been successful. For Zn ferrites, and composite, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra shows that the absorption band of the tetrahedral site at 990 cm−1, and 870 cm−1, respectively. Different bonds in the infrared spectrum match specific function groups and bonding in various chemicals. The vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) results indicates that the composite displays ferrimagnetic behavior. The specific capacity for composite has been found to be 2351 and 1790 F/g using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements, respectively. Higher electrochemical performance of the prepared composites may be helpful for energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Magnetic materials are thought to be an exciting field of research in 21st century. Ferrites are magnetic material composed of iron oxide and metal ions (F
e 2
O 3
, M
n
2 +
, Z
n
2 +
e t c ) .
Ferrites can be multicolored, such as black, brown and grey. The material is made of magnetic oxide, which consists of iron oxide as an essential element. Ferrites have greater resistivity than ferromagnetic material and show higher ferrimagnetism because ferrites have ions on one side and opposite spin on the other [1]. Ferrites have become a promising candidate for industrial use because of their high resistivity, dielectric polarization, switching capabilities and high permeability [2]. Ferrites offer several advantages; they can achieve higher performance in relatively low materials than any other material [3]. One or more bivalent oxides are combined with trivalent iron oxide to form ferrites. Most are stoichiometrically balanced, with bivalent and ferric oxides in a 1:1 ratio, but some are not [4]. Ferrites have various compositions; their magnetic characteristics can vary substantially from one element to the next because their microscopic (atomic) structure is made up of two or more magnetic sub-lattices. Because ferrites are not conducting magnetic media, eddy current and ohmic losses are reduced [5]. Ferrites in tiny particles or thin films can be manufactured utilizing processes such as very high temperature solid-state [6], sol-gel [7], coprecipitation [8], hydrothermal [9], and high energy ball milling [10] methods. At low frequencies, ferrites have a permittivity of thousands, but it drops to roughly 12 to 22 at microwave frequencies due to the crowded arrangement of O2 ions [11]. Ferrites typically have a lower hysteresis or square loop area [12]. ZnFe2O4 is one of the most prominent ferrite binary oxides also with spinel structure, and it is commonly employed as a ferrimagnetism, with magnetic properties that are strongly dependent on the stage of chemical order and cation site occupancy in the materials [13]. The general structural formula is (ZnFe)x [ZnFe]2-xO4. All Zn2+ ions are found in tetrahedral positions (A sites), while all Fe3+ ions are found in octahedral sites (B sites). Heating and mechanical treatment generally cause the emergence of Zn and Fe ion disorder across the tetrahedral and octahedral sites [14]. This in turn leads to a significant variation of the magnetic properties. Such a variation can be distinguished by the distinct temperatures of the antiferromagnetic long-range orders [15].

Carbon is a noble element that appears in a variety of allotropic forms, such as diamond and graphite. It also has a wide range of nanomaterials, such as carbon dots, nanotubes, nanodiamonds, and graphene. Carbon dots (C-dots) are globular in shape and about 10 nm in size. Carbon nanomaterials are cylindrical, containing graphene layers arrayed as stacked cones and plates [16]. Carbon nanofibres are designated carbon nanotubes (graphene layers) are bundled into flawless cylinders [17]. Carbon nanodiamonds have truncated octahedral constructions with diameters ranging from 2 to 8 nm [18]. Graphene is made up of a single layer containing carbon atoms that are organized in a two-dimensional hexagonal arrangement [18]. These materials are used for a wide range of investigations, with spectacular outcomes [19]. C-dots are a novel and versatile family of carbon materials with a nano size range and remarkable luminous and catalytic properties [20]. They have distinct features that are considerably superior and wonderful to those of their contemporaries. They are biocompatible and non-toxic materials used only in bio-imaging probes [21]. They have distinct features that are considerably superior and wonderful to those of their contemporaries. They are biocompatible and non-toxic materials used only in bio-imaging probes of biomedicine, such as bio-sensing, drug carriers, and gene delivery [22], as outstanding theranostic agents [23] for transporting anti-cancer drugs, and in detecting numerous biological and non-biological molecules [24]. C-dots have played an important role in showing outstanding bacterial image, antibacterial action [25], and catalysis [26].

Ferrite-based materials have garnered significant attention in recent years as potential candidates for asymmetric supercapacitors, a versatile energy storage technology that combines the high-power density of supercapacitors with the energy density of batteries. Literature review reveals that numerous studies have explored the unique properties of ferrites for this specific application. Asymmetric supercapacitors typically consist of a high-capacity anode and a high-power-density cathode. Due to their exceptional charge storage capabilities, excellent cycling stability, and environmentally friendly nature, ferrites have emerged as promising candidates for both anodic and cathodic materials in asymmetric supercapacitors [27]. The anode materials in ferrite-based asymmetric supercapacitors are often based on transition metal ferrites, such as Co ferrite (CoFe2O4) or Mn ferrite (MnFe2O4). These materials exhibit high specific capacities due to their multi-valent metal ions and can effectively store energy through redox reactions [28].

On the other hand, the cathode materials in asymmetric supercapacitors are typically based on metal oxide or conductive polymers. Ferrite-based cathodes, such as nickel ferrite (NiFe2O4) or zinc ferrite (ZnFe2O4), have demonstrated promising capacitive properties due to their high electrical conductivity and pseudocapacitance characteristics [29]. Despite the promise of ferrite-based asymmetric supercapacitors, challenges related to optimizing the device's performance, ensuring long-term stability, and cost-effective fabrication remain [30].

The given discussion shows there are still many challenges in the realization of suitable material for asymmetric supercapacitors and other possible applications of ferrites. Therefore, careful substitution of carbon based materials such as C-dots can be used to tune their performance. This study offers a simple hydrothermal approach for producing magnetic core-shell C-dots and its composite ZnFe2O4 hybrid materials. The hybrid material's morphology, microstructure, magnetic, and electrochemical characteristics have been investigated.

Fine particles of Zn ferrites were prepared using the coprecipitation method. The coprecipitation method is the simplest and most common method for synthesizing catalysts and nanoparticles with dimensionless structures. This method is divided into two parts: chemical synthesis in the liquid state, which defines the chemical composition, and post thermal treatment, which controls the crystal structure and morphology [31,32]. In this process, first of all, 50 ml of distilled water was put into two separate beakers of 100 ml. Then 5.404 g of iron chloride and 1.3631 g of zinc chloride (Sigma Aldrich 99.99 %) were mixed with distilled water in separate beakers and stirred continuously. Then, these two solutions were mixed up in 200 ml beakers, and sodium hydroxide was added to the solution drop by drop under constant stirring until the pH noted above 8. After this, the solution was washed many times with ethanol. After washing, the sample was put into the oven for drying at 60
°C
for 11–12 h. After drying, the sample was grinded and then put into a furnace at 700
°C
for 5 h. All moisture was carried out under temperature and saved in a bullet box for characterization and further study.

Fine particles of C-dots were prepared using the hydrothermal method. Hydrothermal synthesis processes are used to create a variety of crystalline metals, iron oxide, and metal nitride nanoparticles [31,32]. In this process, first of all, take 20 ml of distilled water in a beaker of 50 ml and 25 ml milk in a beaker of 50 ml under continuous stirring for 20 min. In that procedure, the solution is mixed up under constant stirring for 15 min, put a mixture into the autoclave and then kept in the oven at 180
°C
for 2 h. The autoclave was removed from the oven when it cooled down to room temperature, and then the solution was washed many times with distilled water and ethanol. Then, the material is put into a vacuum oven for drying at 50
°C
for 18 h. After drying, brown precipitates were achieved of C-dots, which were saved in bullet box for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Zinc Ferrites and carbon dots composite
ce:section.ce:para: Fine particles of C-dots and their composite with Zn ferrites were prepared using the hydrothermal method. In this process, 0.6815 g of ZnCl2 and 1.351 g of FeCl3 were added into separate beakers of 50 ml distilled water and 0.5 g of C-dots into this solution. The rest of the procedure was the same as C-dots.

After the synthesis, the materials were characterized using various experimental techniques like X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) to examine the crystal structure of the materials, morphology, chemical bonding, and magnetic parameters, respectively. The XRD was carried out by Cu-Kα (MiniFlex-III) with wavelength of 1.54 Å and accelerating voltage 40 kV. SEM and EDX were performed with the help of Hitachi SU 1510 machine. KBr pellet method was used to obtain the FTIR spectra. Lakeshore-7407 with applied magnetic field ±5000 Oe was used for magnetic measurements. CV and GCD measurements were made using PAR 273 potentiostat. Three electrode assembly has been used for these measurements in which the active material, Hg/HgO and platinum wire served as working, reference and counter electrode respectively. KOH has been used as an electrolyte in this study.

The prepared Zn ferrites and C-dots composite have been characterized using Cu-Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å. Fig. 1 depicts the diffractogram of Zn ferrite sintered at 700 °C for 5 h, and the results are matched to earlier described results in the literature [33].

Fig. 1 displays the XRD patterns of a series of fully synthesized samples of Zn ferrites/C-dots composite. The reflection planes (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440) and (533) demonstrate that Zn ferrite materials exhibit cubic inverse spinel crystal shapes. The XRD results have no additional peaks. It indicates that the substance seems to have a single phase and that there are no contaminants inside. It was proven by comparing to literature that crystallinity enhanced with high temperature and sintering duration. The peaks move toward a greater angle. The Zn ferrites material resembles the JCPDS card (22–1012). XRD readings of C-dots demonstrated an amorphous shape. C-dots showed a sharp peak at 2
θ
= 20°, which was supported by the previously observed carbon phase. It was also reported that the C-dots broad peak was transformed into a sharp peak due to dramatic dispersion from C-dots into Zn ferrites throughout the composite formation, eventually forming the crystal composite. The main structure of the composite includes significant peaks of C-dots, showing that the required composite has been successfully formed.

Applying Scherer's formula, we determined the crystallite size (D) by using the full width half maxima (
β
) [34]. The formula for crystallite size is given by Equation (1);
(1)
D =
0.9 λ
β
cos
θ
β is full width half maxima for 311, θ is Bragg's angle. The average crystallite size is 119.79 nm.

For single phase samples, the lattice constant (a) was determined using the formula below [35].
(2)
sin 2
θ =
λ 2
4
a 2
(
h 2
+
k 2
+
l 2
)

The Miller Indices of every line are hkl. The following formula can be used to calculate Miller indices.
(3)
d hkl
=
a
h 2
+ k
2 +
l 2
Here, d represents inter-planar spacing.

Unit cell volume can be determined using equation (4) [36];
(4)
V =
a 3

X-ray describes the density of atoms and molecules in a compound's unit cell. The density of X-rays was calculated using the equation below [37];
(5)
D x
=
8 M
N A
a 3
M represents the related sample's molar mass, and N.A. indicates Avogadro's number (6.022x1023).

Dislocation density refers to the number of dislocations per unit volume. The number of lines per unit area calculates dislocation density using the formula below [38].
(6)
δ =
15 ε
aD

Here
ε
represents lattice strain, which is given by Ref. [38];
(7)
ε =
β
4
tan
θ

C-dots, being significantly smaller than the iron oxide lattice, introduce a lattice strain when incorporated into the ferrite structure. This strain typically prompts the lattice to contract as it adapts to accommodate the smaller carbon dot structures, consequently resulting in a decrease in the lattice constant. A decrease in the value of lattice constant for Zn ferrite/C-dots results in lower unit cell volume and higher X-ray density than Zn ferrite. The variations in crystallite size with C-dots are pretty significant as they appreciated a decrease, which could add to the porosity of the materials. Highly porous materials are well known for better electrochemical performance. Considering the above discussion, the variations in dislocation density and lattice strain are quite obvious.

Infrared spectroscopy is being widely used to check the presence of functional groups. Atoms in spinel ferrite materials are arranged in octahedral and tetrahedral sites, respectively. Every divalent metal ion is connected to four oxide ions in regular spinel, but each trivalent metal ion is attached to six oxide ions. Consequently, a unit cell of spinel ferrite contains eight tetrahedral A-sites and sixteen octahedral B-sites.

FTIR spectrum of the prepared materials in terms of ferrite structure, the most significant portion of IR is in the 4000–400 c
m
‐ 1
region. The FTIR spectra of C-dots, Zn ferrites, and composite have been given in Fig. 2 (a) and (2b), and 2(c), respectively. Stretching vibrations at the tetrahedral site may also be seen in our spectra. FTIR spectra of C-dots show vibrational peaks due to C-H bonds in the range 2800–3000 cm−1. The vibrations in the range 1600–1800 cm−1 belong to C═O bonds. The bending vibrations in the range 1400–1600 cm−1 come from C═C vibrations, and bending vibrations due to C-O appear at 1120 cm−1. The bending vibrations at 870 cm−1 may be ascribed as C-H bending vibration. FTIR spectra of the composite show that the C-dots molecular interactions are dominant in the overall molecular structure of the composite. Zn ferrites show tetrahedral sites bending vibrations at 583 cm−1 and octahedral site bending vibrations at 430 cm−1. The tetrahedral and octahedral site bending vibrations shift to 580 cm−1 and 421 cm−1, respectively for composite.

The FTIR spectra results can be used to determine the stretching vibrations (Vo) and to obtain the force constants (Ko) using the formula;
(8)
K
t / o =
4
π 2
V o
2
C 2
m

Results are shown in Table 2
for tetrahedral sites where C is the speed of light and m is the decreased mass (see Table 1). The force constant is higher for Zn ferrite than its composite (see Table 3).

Scanning electron microscopy was used to investigate the surface morphology of ZnF
e 2
O 4
/
C-dots composite nanomaterials and images are presented in Fig. 3 . The ZnFe2O4/C-dots composite images were taken at 500 nm magnification. Micrographs of Zn ferrites sample show the agglomeration of particles, which exhibit a non-uniform spherical shape and small size. C-dots and their composite show globular images with uniform shape and nano size. The particle agglomeration reduction is evident in micrographs. The crystal size follows the structural parameter trend. However, the magnitude differs since grain is made up of crystallites. According to surface morphology, such nanomaterials are trying to form clusters and have a uniform distribution [39].

EDX was used to determine the elemental composition of the prepared samples. Detectors in an EDX machine are calibrated to detect specific energy radiations. The detectors were unable to detect energy below the calibrated value. The EDX spectra of all prepared materials from Fig. 4 (a–c) have been shown. The EDX confirmed the presence of the elemental chemical composition of ZnF
e 2
O 4
/C-dots composite nanoparticles. The carbon peak is clearly visible in the EDX. graph, which should be noted. These are caused by material ashes that form during heat treatment.

Fig. 5 depicts a hysteresis loop of Zn ferrite and Zn ferrite/C-dots composite heat treated at 700 °C for 5 h. The graph reveals the ferromagnetic properties whereby Zn2+ favors the tetrahedral location in Zn ferrite, and Fe3+ ions inhabit the octahedral site. As a result, the order and kind of cations between the interstitial sites determine the magnetic characteristics. The synthesis process has a considerable influence on the distribution of cations. Saturation magnetization, coercivity and remanent magnetization are important magnetic considerations. According to the literature, temperature can affect saturation magnetization (MS). It reduces as the temperature rises. Changes in saturation magnetization can be affected by a multitude of factors, including surface effects, spin/cation redistribution, and oxygen. The hysteresis loop clearly shows that the prepared sample's coercivity is just few hundred Oe, indicating that Zn ferrite to be a soft magnetic material. This material is an ideal choice for microwave and fast switching devices due to its low coercivity [40]. The key factors that influence coercivity are porosity and grain size [40]. The M − H loop has been used to determine various magnetic properties of the sample, such as saturation magnetization (Ms), retentivity (Mr), coercive force (Hc), and the squareness ratio (Mr/Ms) [41].

Ms and Mr of Zn ferrite, and its C-dots composite have values of 49 and 45 emu/g and 5.2 and 4.2 emu/g, respectively. Additionally, the results demonstrate that when C-dots was added in comparison to Zn ferrite, the saturation magnetization (Ms) and remanent magnetization (Mr) values reduced. The decreased Ms in composite material may be caused by the substitute between the magnetic Zn ferrite and non-magnetic C-dots. Zn ferrite exhibited a decreased coercivity (Hc) in comparison to its C-dots composite. The inclusion of non-magnetic carbon is responsible for the composites decreased magnetic response. Still, the composite displays superparamagnetic properties as predicted. Because of its almost low coercivity, the practical use of Zn ferrite/C-dots composite simple recovery and separation is highly advantageous. The M − H patterns show that the hybrid materials can readily distinct from the aqueous dispersion by an external magnetic field, making them recyclable for reuse. The room-temperature magnetic characteristics of non carriers are among the most crucial parameters for their use in biological applications. It is commonly known that directing the distribution of pharmaceuticals by an external magnetic field and transporting loaded drugs to specific locations need the manipulation of magnetic nano-carriers.

Equation (9) has been used to determine the magnetic moment [42] of a sample of Z.F.O., ZFO/C-dots composite;
(9)
n B
(
μ B
)
=
Mw ×
M S
5585
Where Mw, and Ms is molecular weight, saturation magnetization of the sample.
The magnetic moment of ZnFe2O4, C-dots/ZnFe2O4 composite (2.1153) and (0.0967) and coercivity (299) Oe and (270) Oe were observed, respectively.

The anisotropic constant explains the difference between transverse and longitudinal magnetization in an anisotropically magnetised medium. Table 4 summarizes the findings. It is represented by the letter K. The below formulas were used to compute anisotropic constant [43].
(10)
K =
Hc × Ms
0.96

The magnetic anisotropic field represents a hypothetical field capable of aligning all magnetic moments perpendicular towards the easy axis in Table 4 summarizes the findings. The below formula describes the magnetic anisotropic field relationship [43].
(11)
H
K =
2 K
μ °
M S
Where,
μ °
= 4
π ×
10
‐ 7
H M
.
All these parameters follow the Ms and Hc variations trend.

The current spherical shape behavior of the electrochemical characteristics of the produced electrodes In order to learn more about the redox processes taking place at the electrode/electrolyte interface, all of the as-prepared electrodes were put through CV at a 100 mV/s scan rate and GCD characteristics (at 4 A/g current density). A CV of electrodes that were made using 2 M electrolyte is shown in Fig. 6 a and b. CV studies at different scan rates for separate electrodes, such as ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4/C-dots, show discrete oxidation and reduction peaks, which are attributed to ferric (Fe3+) ions, electrochemical processes at the electrode surface [44]. The sample's capacitive properties as well as their ability to function as an electric double-layer capacitive by the addition of C-dots were shown by the curve's little bending from their rectangular shape. ZnO may be responsible for the capacitance mechanism [45].

The GCD at various current densities for the ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4/C-dots electrodes were observed individually in order to better evaluate surface electrochemistry. To determine the charge-discharge time and calculate the cyclic stability of the developed electrode, the GCD experiment was conducted. The prepared electrode's GCD curves are shown in Fig. 7 a and b. The linear and symmetric charge and discharge curves exhibit strong forward and backward response reversibility at varied current densities, which is a characteristic of perfect capacitive behavior [46]. Due to the addition of C-dots to Zn ferrites, the ZnFe2O4@C-dots electrode offers superior electrochemical characteristics over the ZnFe2O4 electrode[47–52].

The specific capacity and specific capacitance measured from CV and GCD curves has been shown in Figs. 8 and 9
, respectively. As estimated by CV measurements, Zn ferrite has a specific capacity of 632 C/g at 3 mV/s. Zn ferrite is combined with C-dots to enhance performance. A higher specific capacity of 1411.15 C/g is seen in ZnFe2O4@C-dots sample. The specific capacity for ZnFe2O4/C-dots has increased due to improvements in surface area, better conductivity, and the special electrical characteristics of C-dots. The specific capacitance of Zn ferrite is 1053.38 F/g, which is lower than that of 2351.93 F/g for ZnFe2O4/C-dots.

By estimating the discharge time for Zn ferrite and ZnFe2O4/C-dots, specific capacity could be calculated using GCD curves. Increased internal resistance may be the cause of the shorter charge-discharge time as current density rises. With a current density of 2 A/g, ZnFe2O4@C-dots has a maximum specific capacity of 1074 C/g which is greater than the Zn ferrite electrode. ZnFe2O4/C-dots improved specific capacity due to its increased surface area and electrical conductivity [53-57].

The specific capacitance was also calculated using the GCD approach. ZnFe2O4/C-dots have the highest specific capacitance value of 1790 F/g. Fig. 9 compares specific capacity and capacitance measurements Zn ferrite and ZnFe2O4/C-dots using CV and GCD.

The coprecipitation and hydrothermal approach were used to synthesize of Zn ferrites and carbon dots composite, respectively. To analyze the material, various characterization methods were employed. The inverse spinel crystal structures have been confirmed by XRD diffraction. The lattice constant (a) has been reduced from 8.19 Å to 8.1 Å. Fourier infrared spectroscopy supported the shift in tetrahedral bond stretching from 990 cm−1 to 870 cm−1 in Zn Ferrites to carbon dots composite. SEM images indicated particle agglomeration and a non-uniform dispersion of particles on the surface. The average particle size decreased due to the increase of carbon dots material in composite form. The EDX investigation revealed that all elements are available in the necessary stoichiometric ratio. According to the M − H graph, the amount of saturation magnetization dropped from 49 to 45 emu/g, as well as the net magnetic moment reduced from 2.1153 to 0.0967 μB with increasing the carbon dots contents in the composite. Electrochemical performance in terms of specific capacity shows encouraging results when using the prepared composites as supercapacitors.

Sulaiman Al-Sulaimi: Methodology, Conceptualization. M. Qamar Jahan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Mukhtar Ahmad: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Zahra Batool: Data curation. Muhammad Hamza: Formal analysis, Data curation. Atiq ur Rehman: Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ghulam Abbas Ashraf: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jin Yang: Formal analysis, Software. Essam A. Al-Ammar: Methodology, Investigation. Saikh Mohammad Wabaidur: Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R492), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. The author S. Al Sulaimi acknowledges University of Nizwa authority for their great support and encouragement in doing this research work.
